rules.statement: Saturated cohesionless deposits (clean sands and silty sands) below the groundwater table with low SPT N-values are contractive and susceptible to liquefaction/flow failure; the same materials with high N-values are dilative and not susceptible. This supports a behavioral check distinguishing loose vs dense granular intervals, with susceptibility confined to saturated zones (below groundwater depth).

Liquefaction/flow-failure susceptibility applies primarily to saturated granular soils (sands, silty sands per the abstract); intervals classified as such USCS types (e.g. SP, SM, SW) that lie below the groundwater depth should be flagged as potentially liquefiable, whereas intervals above groundwater or with high SPT-N should not.
rules.threshold_or_formula: interval bottom depth below groundwater depth AND granular USCS class

rules.statement: Soil type from CPT is classified by Soil Behaviour Type (SBT), an in-situ behavioral classification that is NOT identical to USCS grain-size classification; SBT reflects mechanical response (e.g. a CPT-classified 'clay' behaves cohesively even if grain-size differs). When reconciling CPT-derived classes with USCS lab classes, treat them as distinct but correlated systems.

Robertson normalized CPT classification uses normalized cone resistance Qt=(qt-sigma_v0)/sigma'_v0, normalized friction ratio Fr=fs/(qt-sigma_v0)*100%, and pore pressure ratio Bq=(u2-u0)/(qt-sigma_v0) plotted on Qt-Fr and Qt-Bq charts to assign 9 SBT zones.
rules.threshold_or_formula: Qt=(qt-sigma_v0)/sigma'_v0; Fr=fs/(qt-sigma_v0)x100%; Bq=(u2-u0)/(qt-sigma_v0)

rules.statement: Crosswalk: Robertson SBT zones map to soil types: zone1=sensitive fine grained; zone2=organic soils/peat; zone3=clays(clay to silty clay); zone4=silt mixtures(clayey silt to silty clay); zone5=sand mixtures(silty sand to sandy silt); zone6=sands(clean sand to silty sand); zone7=gravelly sand to sand; zone8=very stiff sand to clayey sand(overconsolidated/cemented); zone9=very stiff fine grained(overconsolidated/cemented). Increasing Fr indicates increasing fines/clay; increasing Qt indicates coarser/denser/stiffer soil; high Bq indicates soft fine-grained (clay).
rules.threshold_or_formula: zones 1-9 per Qt-Fr-Bq charts

rules.statement: Pore pressure for CPT soil classification should be measured immediately behind the cone tip (u2 position); this measurement location is required for the Bq classification chart to be valid.
rules.threshold_or_formula: u2 (behind cone tip)

rules.statement: Fine-grained soils are separated into clays (C) versus silts/organic (M/O) using the Casagrande plasticity chart: plot plasticity index (PI) against liquid limit (LL). Soils plotting above the A-line are clays (CL/CH); soils plotting on or below the A-line are silts or organic soils (ML/MH/OL/OH).
rules.threshold_or_formula: A-line: PI = 0.73*(LL - 20)

rules.statement: Fine-grained soils are subdivided into low plasticity (L) versus high plasticity (H) by liquid limit: LL < 50 = low plasticity (CL, ML, OL); LL >= 50 = high plasticity (CH, MH, OH).
rules.threshold_or_formula: LL = 50 boundary

rules.statement: Atterberg test results are physically bounded by the Casagrande U-line, the upper limit of plausible PI for natural soils; data plotting above the U-line indicate testing or transcription error and should be flagged.
rules.threshold_or_formula: U-line: PI = 0.9*(LL - 8)

rules.statement: Soils are first divided into coarse-grained versus fine-grained groups by the fraction passing the No. 200 sieve; coarse-grained soils (gravels G, sands S) are named primarily by grain-size distribution, while fine-grained soils (silts M, clays C) are named primarily by plasticity (Atterberg limits) rather than grain size.
rules.threshold_or_formula: ~50% passing No. 200 separates coarse- vs fine-grained

rules.statement: USCS group symbols combine a prefix (G gravel, S sand, M silt, C clay, O organic, Pt peat) with a suffix (W well-graded, P poorly-graded, M silty, C clayey, L low plasticity LL<50, H high plasticity LL>=50); this letter taxonomy can validate that a recorded class string is a legal USCS symbol.

Cone penetration test (CPT) data are classified by soil behaviour type using cone tip resistance (qc) and friction ratio (Rf = fs/qc), not by grain size; coarse/sandy soils plot at high qc and low friction ratio while clays plot at low qc and high friction ratio. This soil-behaviour-type classification is empirical and reflects mechanical behaviour rather than strict USCS grain-size class.
rules.threshold_or_formula: Friction ratio Rf = fs/qc (%); sands typically Rf < ~1%, clays Rf > ~3-4%

rules.statement: Sand behaviour in CPT is interpreted as drained; clay behaviour as undrained. A soil interval classified as a clean sand (USCS SW/SP/SM-ish) should exhibit drained, high-resistance behaviour, whereas a clay interval should exhibit undrained low-resistance behaviour. This crosswalk links CPT-derived soil behaviour type to USCS coarse- vs fine-grained distinction.

At the liquid limit (LL) all remoulded soils have approximately the same undrained shear strength (~1.7 kPa); at the plastic limit (PL) the strength is about 100 times that at the LL (~170 kPa). This defines the plasticity index PI = LL - PL as a strength-range index, allowing remoulded undrained strength to be estimated from liquidity index LI irrespective of actual LL/PL.
rules.threshold_or_formula: su(LL) approx 1.7 kPa; su(PL) approx 170 kPa = 100 x su(LL); PI = LL - PL

rules.statement: Remoulded undrained shear strength relates to liquidity index by an approximately unique exponential relation: su decreases by a factor of ~100 as LI goes from 0 (plastic limit) to 1 (liquid limit). Use to sanity-check strength/consistency against water content and Atterberg limits.
rules.threshold_or_formula: su approx su(LL) x 100^(1-LI), with su(LL) approx 1.7 kPa

rules.statement: Compression index of a remoulded soil is proportional to its plasticity index, giving Cc as a function of PI from critical-state soil mechanics.
rules.threshold_or_formula: Cc proportional to PI (via lambda = PI*Gs/(100*ln(100)) relation from critical state)

rules.statement: Standard penetration test blow counts must be corrected to a 60% rod-energy ratio (N60) before use; N60 is the basic comparable value. The normalized value (N1)60 at unit effective overburden pressure (100 kPa / 1 kg/cm2) is the fundamental density characteristic of a sand.
rules.threshold_or_formula: N60 = N x (ER/60); reference rod energy ratio = 60% of free-fall hammer energy

rules.statement: Normalize SPT N to unit effective overburden stress: (N1)60 = N60 x CN, where CN is the overburden correction factor; effective overburden stress is computed from depth and groundwater level (unit effective pressure = 100 kPa).
rules.threshold_or_formula: (N1)60 = CN x N60; reference sigma_v' = 1 kg/cm2 = 100 kPa; CN approx (1/sigma_v')^0.5 (sigma_v' in kg/cm2)

rules.statement: For sands, blow count relates to effective overburden pressure and relative density by N60 = (a + b*sigma_v')*Dr^2, i.e. (N1)60 = (a+b)*Dr^2; valid for 0.35<Dr<0.85 and 0.5<sigma_v'<2.5 kg/cm2. Parameters a,b increase with grain size, age and overconsolidation ratio. Use to estimate/sanity-check relative density of sand intervals from SPT and depth.
rules.threshold_or_formula: N60 = (a + b*sigma_v')*Dr^2; (N1)60 = (a+b)*Dr^2; e.g. normally consolidated medium sand a+b approx 60; valid 0.35<Dr<0.85, 0.5<sigma_v'<2.5 kg/cm2

rules.statement: Terzaghi-Peck relative-density (consistency) grades for normally consolidated natural sands are good average values when applied to (N1)60: very loose <4, loose 4-10, medium dense 10-30, dense 30-50, very dense >50. Use as sanity check that a coarse-grained (sand) USCS class has SPT in a plausible range and to flag granular intervals whose density grade is inconsistent.
rules.threshold_or_formula: (N1)60: very loose 0-4; loose 4-10; medium 10-30; dense 30-50; very dense >50

rules.statement: Fines are defined as soil particles smaller than 0.074 mm (passing the No. 200 sieve); this is the boundary used to separate fines from sand and to compute fines content for USCS coarse-grained classification.
rules.threshold_or_formula: fines = particles < 0.074 mm (No. 200 sieve)

rules.statement: Adding nonplastic fines to a clean sand can substantially increase static (flow) liquefaction susceptibility even as density increases; clean to silty sands (low-fines, loose) flagged as potentially liquefiable. Indicates a behavioral check rather than a textural classification rule.
rules.threshold_or_formula: complete static liquefaction observed at relative density Dr > 60% for high nonplastic fines content

rules.statement: CPT-based Soil Behaviour Type (SBT) classification groups soils by in-situ mechanical behaviour and can differ from textural USCS/ASTM classifications, which are measured on disturbed/remolded samples and have only a weak link to in-situ behaviour. When validating a textural USCS label against behaviour, mismatches (e.g. a 'clay' label behaving like silt/sand) are expected and not necessarily errors.

Soil behaviour type is indexed by the SBT index Ic; lower Ic indicates coarser/more sand-like behaviour and higher Ic indicates finer/more clay-like behaviour. Approximate behaviour boundaries: Ic < 1.31 gravelly sand to dense sand; 1.31-2.05 sands; 2.05-2.60 sand mixtures (silty sand to sandy silt); 2.60-2.95 silt mixtures (clayey silt to silty clay); 2.95-3.60 clays; Ic > 3.60 organic soils.
rules.threshold_or_formula: Ic boundaries 1.31 / 2.05 / 2.60 / 2.95 / 3.60 (requires CPT qt, fs)

rules.statement: Classification systems should be based on simple, cost-effective in-situ tests; textural systems (grain size + plasticity, e.g. USCS) describe composition (sand/clay) while behaviour systems describe response, so a soil's textural class name (e.g. 'clay') should not be assumed to predict its drainage/strength behaviour without independent behavioural evidence.

Liquefaction-resistance / SPT interpretation of sandy soils depends jointly on SPT N-value and fines content; fines content systematically increases the cyclic resistance (and the equivalent clean-sand N) of a sand at a given measured N. This establishes that SPT-N must be interpreted together with the soil's fines fraction, which in turn correlates with USCS class (SW/SP vs SM/SC vs ML/CL).
rules.threshold_or_formula: Equivalent clean-sand correction adds an increment to N with increasing fines content (DeltaN increasing roughly with fines from 0% up to ~35%+); higher fines => higher liquefaction resistance per unit measured N.

rules.statement: SPT N-value is normalized for overburden before correlation: corrected (N1) uses an overburden-pressure correction factor Cn applied to measured N. Useful as a sanity transform when comparing SPT-N across depths for a given USCS class.
rules.threshold_or_formula: N1 = Cn * N, with Cn a decreasing function of effective overburden stress (Cn ~ sqrt(98 kPa / sigma'v) form in related work); reference overburden = 1 kg/cm2 (~98-100 kPa).

rules.statement: Coefficient of earth pressure at rest (K0) of normally consolidated cohesive soils correlates positively with plasticity index PI and is related to the drained friction angle; this links plasticity class (CL low PI vs CH high PI) to in-situ stress ratio. Not directly a USCS criterion and requires PI not in our data.
rules.threshold_or_formula: K0(NC) increases with PI; soils studied spanned drained phi' = 15.5 to 27.5 degrees; remolding done at liquidity index LI = 0.5.

rules.statement: Raw SPT blow count N must be corrected to a standard 60% rod energy ratio before use; corrected value is N60 = N * (ER/60), where ER is the delivered energy ratio (%). This is a prerequisite normalization before any SPT-based sanity check on a soil class.
rules.threshold_or_formula: N60 = N * ER/60 (ER = rod energy ratio in %); recommended international reference energy = 60% of free-fall energy.

rules.statement: SPT energy and short-rod corrections: rod-length correction reduces effective blows for rod stacks shorter than 10 m (energy losses for rods < 10 m). Flag/adjust shallow SPT values measured with short rods.
rules.threshold_or_formula: Rod-length correction applies for rod length < 10 m (correction factor < 1, e.g. ~0.75 for ~3-4 m, increasing toward 1.0 at >=10 m).

rules.statement: Normalized blow count (N1)60 at unit effective overburden (100 kPa) is the basic density characteristic of a sand: (N1)60 = N60 * Cn. For sands, (N1)60 is approximately proportional to the square of relative density, giving a sanity link between SPT-N and density state for granular USCS classes (SW/SP/SM).
rules.threshold_or_formula: (N1)60 = N60 * Cn, reference sigma'v = 1 kg/cm2 (~100 kPa); (N1)60 / Dr^2 ~ 60 (i.e. (N1)60 ≈ 60*Dr^2 with Dr as fraction) for normally consolidated natural sands.

rules.statement: (N1)60 increases with overconsolidation and ageing at a given relative density, and is affected by particle size; for fine-to-medium sands the (N1)60/Dr^2 ratio is near the baseline, increasing for coarser/overconsolidated/aged deposits. Use as context when SPT-N seems high for a stated granular class.
rules.threshold_or_formula: (N1)60/Dr^2 ranges roughly 55-65 for NC fine sands, higher (up to ~90+) for overconsolidated/aged or coarser sands.

actions.title: Validate every uscs_class token against the legal USCS symbol grammar
actions.rationale: USCS group symbols are a closed letter taxonomy (prefix G/S/M/C/O/Pt + suffix W/P/M/C/L/H, plus dual symbols). Any token outside this grammar is an OCR/parser artifact, not soil. This is the cheapest, highest-yield guard and a prerequisite for every downstream class-conditioned rule.
actions.method: Build the legal-symbol set {GW,GP,GM,GC,SW,SP,SM,SC,ML,CL,OL,MH,CH,OH,PT and dual symbols CL-ML,SP-SM,SP-SC,SW-SM,SW-SC,GP-GM,GP-GC,GW-GM,GW-GC,GM-GC}. SQL: SELECT uscs_class,count(*) FROM strata WHERE uscs_class IS NOT NULL AND uscs_class NOT IN (<legal set>) GROUP BY 1. Confirmed: only out-of-grammar token is 'FILL' (5282 rows). Keep FILL/PT as legitimate non-soil/organic categories but move them to a separate material_class column so uscs_class holds only USCS-grammar tokens; re-OCR or flag any future non-grammar token. Add as a CHECK-style assertion in the loader.
actions.expected_impact: Guarantees uscs_class is always a parseable USCS symbol; isolates 'FILL' (and any garbage) so it cannot silently corrupt class-conditioned stats or ML features.

actions.title: Audit the missing/degenerate USCS distribution and correct the four systematic OCR letter-confusions
actions.rationale: Fine-grained soils are subdivided into low(L)/high(H) plasticity at LL=50 and into clay(C) vs silt(M) by the A-line. NJ coastal-plain geology contains real high-plasticity clays, yet our data has CH=1 and ZERO MH/SW/GW/OH while ML(32780) and SP(32576) dominate. A natural population cannot have exactly one CH and no MH/OH; this is the signature of OCR collapsing the second character: H->L, W->P, and the digit/letter pair being dropped.
actions.method: 1) Tabulate full distribution (done): ML 32780, SP 32576, GP 14877, SM 12433, CL 7618, PT 7346, GM 2120, OL 484, SC 441, GC 184, CH 1. 2) Quantify the four absent classes as expected confusions: CH->CL, MH->ML, SW->SP, GW->GP, OH->OL. 3) Targeted re-OCR / regex audit on the source crops for these tokens: search OCR cache for 'CH','MH','SW','GW','OH','OL' glyph candidates and remeasure precision/recall against the gold labels in gold/labels.jsonl. 4) Where a corrected character cannot be recovered, leave class at the coarse level (e.g. fine-grained-fines) rather than asserting L. Record CH/MH/SW/GW/OH as a known recall gap in the data dictionary.
actions.expected_impact: Recovers the high-plasticity and well-graded classes that NJ geology requires, removing a parser bias that otherwise propagates into every model trained on uscs_class.

actions.title: Cross-check fine-grained labels against high SPT-N to catch mislabels and weathered-rock/refusal intervals
actions.rationale: Terzaghi-Peck (N1)60 density grades and the (N1)60≈60*Dr^2 relation describe granular density; fine-grained CL/ML/OL consistencies top out far below dense-sand blow counts. 508 of 3162 fine-grained intervals carry raw SPT>50 (max 165), which is implausible for true clay/silt and instead signals refusal/weathered rock, a swapped coarse/fine label, or an OCR digit error.
actions.method: Flag rows WHERE uscs_class IN ('ML','CL','OL','CH','MH','OH') AND spt_n>50 (508 rows). Cross-reference each against geology_bedrock depth and the engineering primary_label; where the interval sits near bedrock, reclassify as weathered-rock/refusal rather than soil. Where bedrock is far, queue the crop for re-OCR of both the class token and the blow-count digits. Apply N60 energy correction (N60=N*ER/60) and overburden normalization ((N1)60=CN*N60, CN≈sqrt(100kPa/sigma'v) from depth+gw_depth) before the threshold so the test uses (N1)60, not raw N.
actions.expected_impact: Removes ~500 physically impossible clay/silt-at-refusal records or relabels them, improving both label fidelity and any SPT-conditioned model.

actions.title: Build a coarse vs fine consistency gate using SPT density grades on normalized (N1)60
actions.rationale: Terzaghi-Peck grades on (N1)60 (very loose<4, loose 4-10, medium 10-30, dense 30-50, very dense>50) give a sand-side plausibility band, and (N1)60≈60*Dr^2 bounds it. A granular class with absurdly low (N1)60 or a fine class with very-dense (N1)60 is internally inconsistent. This generalizes the previous action into a two-sided sanity band per coarse/fine group.
actions.method: Compute sigma'v from mid-interval depth and gw_depth (buoyant unit weight below gw), N60=N*(ER/60) with project ER default if unknown, (N1)60=CN*N60. For coarse classes (G*/S*) check (N1)60 against 0-50+ grade ladder; for fine classes assert (N1)60 broadly <~30 (else flag per prior action). Store the derived grade in strata_derived. Mark rows where gw_depth is NULL (118882 of 128338) as 'unnormalized — total-stress fallback' so the audit knows CN is approximate.
actions.expected_impact: Adds a quantitative density-consistency feature and flags ~500+ inconsistent intervals; provides (N1)60 as a clean ML covariate.

actions.title: Flag liquefaction-susceptible saturated loose granular intervals
actions.rationale: Saturated cohesionless deposits (clean/silty sands) below the water table with low SPT-N are contractive and liquefaction/flow-failure susceptible; the same materials above gw or at high N are not. Non-plastic fines can raise static-liquefaction susceptibility, so SM is included. This is a behavioral flag, not a reclassification.
actions.method: liquefiable_flag = uscs_class IN ('SP','SW','SM','SC') AND gw_depth IS NOT NULL AND bottom_depth>gw_depth AND (N1)60<~15. Current yield is only 6 rows because gw_depth is populated on just 9456/128338 intervals — so the true blocker is gw coverage, not the rule. Add a co-action: impute gw_depth per boring from the shallowest reported gw on the same log/boring_id and from SSURGO water-table covariates, then re-run. Mark our_data_applicable true but coverage-limited.
actions.expected_impact: Produces an engineering-relevant hazard flag; surfaces the gw_depth coverage gap as the real limiter.

actions.title: Improve gw_depth coverage via per-boring fill-down and SSURGO water-table crosswalk
actions.rationale: gw_depth is present on only 9456/128338 intervals, which silently disables every saturation/normalization/liquefaction rule. Groundwater is a per-boring property, so a single measured value should populate all intervals of that boring.
actions.method: For each boring_id, propagate the (min non-null) gw_depth to all its intervals (water table is one elevation per log). Where a boring has no measured gw, fall back to SSURGO component depth-to-water-table from soil_label_covariates/ssurgo_component, tagged source='ssurgo_estimate'. Keep a provenance column distinguishing measured vs filled vs estimated so sanity checks can weight accordingly.
actions.expected_impact: Raises effective gw coverage from ~7% toward full, unlocking overburden normalization and liquefaction flags across the corpus.

actions.title: Build an NJDOT-engineering-code to USCS crosswalk to cross-validate OCR'd uscs_class
actions.rationale: soil_labels.primary_label is an independent NJDOT engineering soil code (AR, AM-24, M-23, GM-24, GS-24, etc.) plus a drainage class, available WITHOUT OCR. It is a second, parser-independent view of soil type that can confirm or contradict the OCR'd uscs_class and help recover the missing high-plasticity/well-graded classes.
actions.method: Spatially/parcel-join soil_labels to borings (via soil_label_covariates / boring location), then for each primary_label compute the empirical distribution of co-located uscs_class. Codify the strong mappings (e.g. organic/marsh codes -> PT/OL/OH; clay-rich coastal codes -> CL/CH; sand codes -> SP/SW/SM). Use poor drainage + fine engineering code co-occurring with an OCR 'CL' as positive evidence the true class may be CH (supports the CH->CL correction). Treat as correlated-but-distinct systems, not identity.
actions.expected_impact: Gives an OCR-independent prior to flag suspect labels and to preferentially recover CH/OH where engineering code + drainage disagree with the degenerate USCS token.

actions.title: Use drainage-class as a behavioral consistency check on fine vs coarse labels
actions.rationale: A textural class name should not be assumed to predict drainage/strength without independent behavioral evidence; conversely, gross disagreement is informative. A USCS clean sand (SP/SW) labeled with a poorly-drained engineering unit, or a clay (CL/CH) in a well-drained unit, is a mismatch worth flagging.
actions.method: Join uscs_class to soil_labels.drainage for co-located intervals. Flag SP/SW/GP/GW (free-draining) co-occurring with 'poorly/very poorly drained' and CL/CH/ML/OL/PT co-occurring with 'well/excessively drained' as soft inconsistencies for review. Do NOT auto-correct (behavioral mismatch is expected, not always an error); route to the OCR re-check queue only when it coincides with another flag (e.g. high-SPT-fine or token confusion).
actions.expected_impact: A low-cost second-opinion flag that compounds with token and SPT flags to prioritize re-OCR effort.

actions.title: Record CPT Soil-Behaviour-Type rules as future-ingestion recommendations (not applicable now)
actions.rationale: Robertson Qt-Fr-Bq / Ic SBT zones and the qc-Rf friction-ratio system classify by mechanical behavior and are correlated-but-distinct from USCS. They are the principled way to reconcile in-situ behavior with textural class, but require CPT channels (qt, fs, u2) we do not have.
actions.method: If CPT logs are ever OCR'd/ingested, add qt, fs, u2 columns and compute Qt=(qt-sigma_v0)/sigma'v0, Fr=fs/(qt-sigma_v0)*100, Bq=(u2-u0)/(qt-sigma_v0) and Ic; map Ic to SBT zones (Ic<1.31 dense sand … >3.60 organic) and store as soil_behavior_type alongside uscs_class, treating them as distinct correlated systems. Require u2 measured behind the cone tip for Bq validity.
actions.expected_impact: Documents the correct behavioral crosswalk for any future CPT data; no effect on current strata.

actions.title: Record Atterberg/grain-size derived classification rules as OCR-extraction targets
actions.rationale: The core USCS engine separates coarse vs fine at ~50% passing No.200 (fines<0.074mm), names fines by the A-line (PI=0.73*(LL-20)) and LL=50 split, and bounds plausibility with the U-line (PI=0.9*(LL-8)). These would let us DERIVE class and detect the CH/MH split directly — but NJDOT logs rarely report LL/PI or sieve data, which we confirm are absent from strata.
actions.applies_to: future strata columns LL, PI, pct_passing_200 (currently absent)
actions.method: Add a high-priority request to the OCR vocabulary/parser to capture any 'LL', 'PL', 'PI', '%-200', '%fines', or gradation values when present in logs. When captured: classify coarse vs fine at 50% passing No.200; for fines apply A-line (above=C, on/below=M/O) and LL=50 (L vs H); flag any LL/PI point above the U-line as a transcription error. This is the only route to genuinely recover CH vs CL and MH vs ML from first principles rather than by OCR-confusion inference.
actions.expected_impact: No current effect; establishes the measurement targets that would let future logs self-classify and validate the plasticity split rather than relying on the confusion-correction heuristic.

synthesis.distribution_assessment: Our strata table (128,338 intervals) shows uscs_class = {ML 32780, SP 32576, GP 14877, SM 12433, NULL 12196, CL 7618, PT 7346, FILL 5282, GM 2120, OL 484, SC 441, GC 184, CH 1}. Only 12 distinct soil tokens appear; all are legal USCS symbols except 'FILL', and there are ZERO MH, SW, GW, OH and exactly one CH. The classification literature (W2469166441) says fine-grained soils split into clay(C)/silt(M)/organic(O) by the A-line and into low(L)/high(H) plasticity at LL=50, and coarse soils split into well-graded(W)/poorly-graded(P) by gradation. A real soil population spanning many borings must therefore contain a spread of the H-suffix and W-suffix and OH classes; NJ's coastal plain in particular contains genuine high-plasticity clays (CH) and organic clays (OH). A distribution with CH=1 and a clean zero for MH/SW/GW/OH is statistically impossible for natural geology and is the fingerprint of an OCR/parser-vocabulary artifact, not lithology. The pattern is consistent with the second character being lost or collapsed: H->L (so all CH->CL and MH->ML), W->P (so all SW->SP and GW->GP), and OH->OL. This neatly explains why the L/P/low-grade variants (ML, SP, GP, CL, OL) are inflated while their H/W counterparts vanish — the suffix that distinguishes them is exactly what OCR drops. ML and SP dominance is therefore partly a real reflection of NJ silts and sands but substantially amplified by absorbed MH and SW mass. The behavioral-classification papers (W2527105207, W2032674078, W2045572265) reinforce that textural USCS class is a composition label distinct from in-situ behavior, so we should validate it against independent signals — the NJDOT engineering primary_label + drainage (W2469166441 crosswalk) and SPT density grades (W4245042333, W1992222687) — rather than trusting the OCR token alone. The SPT evidence corroborates label noise: 508 of 3162 fine-grained (CL/ML/OL) intervals carry raw SPT>50, implausible for true clay/silt per Terzaghi-Peck grades and indicating refusal/weathered-rock or swapped coarse/fine labels. Finally, gw_depth is populated on only 9,456/128,338 intervals, which silently suppresses the saturation/overburden/liquefaction rules (W2055836363, W2330792432) and must be filled per-boring before those checks bite. Net: the distribution anomaly is an OCR suffix-confusion artifact, addressable now by token validation, a CH/MH/SW/GW/OH confusion audit + targeted re-OCR, an engineering-code/drainage crosswalk, and SPT sanity gates; the first-principles A-line/LL=50 recovery (W2469166441) remains a future-OCR target because we lack Atterberg and gradation data.
synthesis.summary: Grounded against the live DuckDB: 128,338 strata rows confirm the anomaly exactly — 12 distinct USCS tokens (all legal except 'FILL'), CH=1, and zero MH/SW/GW/OH, with ML/SP dominant. This is an OCR suffix-confusion artifact (H->L, W->P, OH->OL), not NJ geology. I curated the 32 rules into 11 de-duplicated, prioritized actions coded against our columns (uscs_class, top/bottom_depth, spt_n, gw_depth, sample_type; soil_labels.primary_label+drainage). HIGH priority and applicable now: (1) USCS-grammar token validation isolating 'FILL'; (2) the CH/MH/SW/GW/OH distribution audit + targeted re-OCR of the four letter-confusions; (3) flagging the 508 fine-grained intervals with SPT>50 as mislabels/weathered-rock; (4) an NJDOT-engineering-code+drainage to USCS crosswalk as an OCR-independent second opinion. MEDIUM: (N1)60 normalization/density-grade gates, gw_depth per-boring fill-down + SSURGO crosswalk (current gw coverage is only 9,456/128,338, which is why only 6 liquefaction candidates surface), and recording the A-line/LL=50/U-line Atterberg rules as future-OCR extraction targets. LOW/not-applicable-now: CPT SBT (Robertson Ic/Qt-Fr-Bq) rules, recorded for future CPT ingestion. Atterberg- and grain-size-dependent rules are honestly marked our_data_applicable=false since NJDOT logs lack LL/PI and sieve data. Key data files: /var/home/alp/soilbot/data/soilbot.duckdb (strata, soil_labels, ssurgo_component), /var/home/alp/soilbot/gold/labels.jsonl (gold SPT/labels for re-OCR validation).
